shortTitle: Pov: Your Life When You Get Rich

content.text: Congratulations. You did it. The raise, the venture, the shrewd investments—all of it adds up. You are rich now. Not lottery-winning rich, not comfortably upper-middle-class rich, but real, can’t-hide-it, unmistakably rich. No one around you even comes close. You make more in a month than your parents made in years. Every time you check your bank app, your net worth is another comma higher. At some point, the number stops seeming real. It’s just more zeroes every day.
content.imageDescription: A dramatic close-up of your hands holding a smartphone, bank app open, displaying a balance with multiple commas. The background is blurred, emphasizing the growing numbers, your reflection faintly visible in the screen.

content.text: At first, it’s thrilling. You treat your parents to dinner at the steakhouse the family never bothered to look at when you were a kid because why torture yourself with the impossible?
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of you and your parents dressed up, walking into a luxurious steakhouse under glowing lights, a valet opening the door. Your parents seem a bit nervous but excited.

content.text: You wave the bill away with a card the waiter has to double check, just to make sure it isn’t fake.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of your hand holding an elegant credit card out to a surprised waiter, who inspects the card, your family watching eagerly from the table.

content.text: Your sister snaps a photo. Your dad grins at you over his beer. His hand shakes a little as he sips. He doesn’t say anything, but you know exactly what he’s thinking. You can finally take care of them, forever.
content.imageDescription: Snapshot-style frame of your sister holding up her phone to take a family photo, your dad smiling proudly at you, beer glass in his hand with a slight tremor. You all share a moment of happiness.

content.text: And you do. You buy your mom the car she always glanced at in magazines, never out loud. You pay off your parents’ mortgage. You hand your brother a check with more zeroes than he’s ever seen and tell him to chase his dream.
content.imageDescription: Three-panel montage: Your mom tearfully embraces a new car in a driveway; in an office, you hand your parents a paid-off mortgage document; you pass your brother a check, his eyes wide with disbelief.

content.text: Friends start calling. College classmates you haven’t seen since freshman year pop up in your DMs. Some are direct—congrats, dinner’s on you, right?
content.imageDescription: Over-the-shoulder view of your phone screen lighting up with messages and social media notifications from old friends and classmates, their profile pics and texts popping up rapidly.

content.text: You laugh and book a table for twelve at the best restaurant in the city. It’s easy. Cheaper than a single hour of your portfolio’s growth.
content.imageDescription: Bird’s-eye shot of a lavish dining table set for twelve at an upscale restaurant, reservations being made on your phone while you relax, smiling.

content.text: You order bottles that get uncorked with a flourish. Laughter echoes off the windows. You pick up the whole bill. At the end of the night, you get a round of applause. For a second, you are at the center of everything. And you like it.
content.imageDescription: Joyful scene: friends clinking glasses, a server uncorking wine, you signing the bill with a flourish as everyone cheers and claps. You beam at the attention.

content.text: You text your closest friend, inside joke from high school, the kind that only makes sense to you two, and he texts back instantly. For a week, you feel unstoppable.
content.imageDescription: Dimly lit room, you smiling down at your phone after sending an inside joke. The screen flashes a quick, laughing reply from your best friend. You look genuinely happy.

content.text: But more calls come. Cousins you’ve lost touch with. Old co-workers who watched you struggle at your first job suddenly want to catch up. A friend of a friend slides into your inbox—he has a business idea and you’re the only person he trusts.
content.imageDescription: Your phone overflowing with texts, DMs, and emails—some familiar names, some vague or unknown. Notifications pile up, each message wanting something from you.

content.text: You say yes, once, twice, then lose count. People seem to multiply around you. Everyone wants a little. Some want a lot. Some want to trade memories for money, as if you owe them for every time they spotted you for lunch in college. It’s subtle at first—jokes about how you could buy the bar, shy little hints dropped into every conversation.
content.imageDescription: At a crowded gathering, people lean in close, some laughing, others dropping sly hints about money. You stand in the center, overwhelmed but smiling, everyone vying for your attention.

content.text: You move. Your apartment—the one you worked overtime to afford—suddenly feels too small for your life now. You buy a place with floor-to-ceiling windows, views of the skyline and a balcony big enough to host everyone you care about.
content.imageDescription: A sweeping view from inside your new high-rise apartment, showing enormous windows looking out over a city skyline. The empty spaciousness contrasts with moving boxes stacked near the wall.

content.text: You spend a month picking furniture you never sit in. You commission a walk-in closet, think about a wine cellar. Someone tells you that’s what rich people do.
content.imageDescription: Montage panels: you browsing chic home décor at a fancy showroom, approving blueprints for a vast walk-in closet and a future wine cellar, looking both curious and bemused.

content.text: With money comes options. You trade your six-year-old sedan for a gleaming sports car you’ve only ever seen in movies. The dealership manager shakes your hand a little too enthusiastically.
content.imageDescription: Outside a luxury car dealership: you receive keys from an overly eager manager next to a brand-new sports car, your old car parked off to the side.

content.text: Your friends pile in, grinning, snapping selfies, beaming as you tear down the freeway.
content.imageDescription: Action shot from inside the sports car: friends crammed in, laughing and snapping selfies with the city whizzing past through open windows. You grip the wheel, thrilled.

content.text: You buy jackets that cost as much as rent and sneakers you’ll never wear outside because it might rain. Ordering the appetizer and dessert is standard now, the only real decision is which.
content.imageDescription: Closet overflowing with luxury labels, price tags visible; on a table at a trendy restaurant, multiple high-end appetizers and desserts waiting to be eaten.

content.text: People notice. Your boss asks, flat voice, if your work will still get your attention. The team calls you "big shot" behind your back, some of it is fond, some jealous, some both.
content.imageDescription: In the office: your boss stands in your doorway with a neutral expression, your coworkers glance over and whisper as you walk past, some amused, others resentful.

content.text: Your friends hesitate before suggesting the cheap brunch spots you always loved. One day, you walk in and no one’s there. They rearranged plans without you. It stings, but you shrug it off. You convince yourself it’s just scheduling.
content.imageDescription: You enter a cozy, familiar café, only to find empty tables and no friends. You stand at the entrance, mask disappointment with a forced smile, glancing at your phone.

content.text: Lunches turn to meetings. Meetings turn to golf invites, then galas. You buy the suit, the watch, the shoes, the club membership.
content.imageDescription: Quick montage: you sit at formal meetings, shake hands on a manicured golf course, pose in a tuxedo at a glittering gala. Accessories and luxury are on display.

content.text: Out of nowhere, you are shaking hands with people who laugh too hard at your jokes. They hand you drinks before you ask. You’re networking now, making connections.
content.imageDescription: At a posh cocktail party, you mingle, effortless smile plastered as well-dressed strangers react with exaggerated laughter and offer you drinks on trays.

content.text: You start hearing about deals you never knew existed. You start investing in things that don’t even have websites yet. Everyone tells you you’re a genius. You start to believe them.
content.imageDescription: Conference table with secretive business documents, a sleek tablet showing graphs, people around you clapping you on the back and looking impressed.

content.text: You see less of the people you knew before. Friends start to ask what’s new with an edge in their voice. Your phone rings but you stop picking up, unsure if it’s another cousin or a contractor or a stranger wanting to pitch you on the next big thing. You tell yourself you’re just busy, that it’s temporary.
content.imageDescription: You sit alone at a pristine kitchen counter, your phone buzzing repeatedly on the countertop as you ignore the calls, a hint of sadness on your face.

content.text: You start traveling. Paris for a weekend. Skiing in Switzerland because everyone at the club says that’s where you should go.
content.imageDescription: Montage: you stand with suitcase by the Eiffel Tower, then zoom down a snowy mountain slope in the Alps, everything high-end and picture-perfect.

content.text: Your Instagram becomes a travel log of places your friends from school can only double tap. You eat at restaurants so exclusive that reservations are lottery tickets. You get used to having your coat taken, your car parked, your seat pulled out. The staff brings you things before you ask. You learn not to be surprised.
content.imageDescription: Over-the-shoulder view of your phone: your Instagram feed overflowing with travel and fancy food posts; jump cut to a staff member pulling out your chair at a white-tablecloth restaurant as you sit, unfazed.

content.text: But then, you sit alone in a suite the size of your parents’ house. It’s silent. You call your mom, but she sounds distracted, busy with something small, something familiar. She asks if you’re eating well, if you’re sleeping. You tell her yes. She tells you that your brother still hasn’t cashed that check. He says he doesn’t want your money, that you’re… different now. He still fixes his own car in the driveway. He doesn’t answer your texts.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of a lonely, lavish hotel suite with you sitting on the edge of a king-size bed, phone pressed to your ear. Through a window, city lights glow. The emotion is isolation amid luxury.

content.text: At a party, someone laughs at a story you’re telling, a story about growing up broke, and suddenly you wonder if you’re the punchline or just the spectacle. You listen close, trying to guess what they want—the person or the access. Your jokes get less funny. You stop sharing anything personal. You lock your phone with Face ID, then change the lock again.
content.imageDescription: Party scene: you telling a story, guests laughing a little too loudly. A shift in your face as realization dawns. Cut to you alone in a hallway, locking your phone, wary.

content.text: Someone you’ve never met sues you. A former acquaintance says you promised him a share in a business you barely remember mentioning.
content.imageDescription: Close-up on an official looking envelope: legal papers inside. Flashback overlay of a vague handshake in a crowded room, faces blurred in memory.

content.text: You call your lawyer and he tells you not to worry, it’s what happens when people know you have money. You settle just to avoid the headlines, and discover that’s how it goes: money solves the problem, but everyone seems a little colder after.
content.imageDescription: Dim office with your lawyer seated across from you, reassuring. You sign a settlement document, looking resigned. The background is chilly, devoid of warmth.

content.text: You offer a donation to your alma mater. A big one. The school invites you to speak at graduation. Your old professors treat you like an honorary guest. For an hour you’re the pride of the community.
content.imageDescription: Large auditorium: you at a podium in cap and gown, professors smiling behind you, a crowd applauding. Your name projected on a big screen.

content.text: But then, a parent of a student tells you quietly at a reception: people think you forgot where you came from. The new wealth is rubbing some people the wrong way. The university names a bench after you anyway.
content.imageDescription: Post-ceremony reception: A parent leans in to whisper with a slightly judgmental expression. Nearby, a newly unveiled bench bears your name in gold letters.

content.text: Birthdays change. Your family expects flights to you or from you. You pay for it all, because you don’t want to seem cheap. The old traditions fade into catered dinners, awkward silences, presents more expensive but less thoughtful. Everyone seems to talk about things, not memories.
content.imageDescription: Table set for a catered birthday dinner in a modern apartment, family present but quiet, tension in the air as expensive wrapped gifts sit unopened.

content.text: Your cousin avoids you now. At holidays, he lectures about money ruining everything. You listen, but stop arguing. Maybe he’s right. Maybe you are different now.
content.imageDescription: At a holiday get-together, your cousin stands and gestures passionately during an argument about money, while you listen in silence, distant from the rest of the group.

content.text: You date, but every first impression feels like a test. You show up and the other person already knows your job, your car, your net worth. At some point, they slip and mention a detail you never shared. Suddenly, you’re not sure if they want you or the lifestyle.
content.imageDescription: Romantic restaurant: you on a date, your companion asks a question that surprises you, a hint of suspicion in your expression as you lean back.

content.text: You try going back to old haunts, but can’t relax. You tip too much. You get stares. The bartender greets you by name, but it’s not the same.
content.imageDescription: Corner of a dive bar: you sit uncomfortably at the bar, bartender nods politely, nearby patrons cast glances your way, your generous tip left beside your half-empty drink.

content.text: The people who still remember you before the money seem to be walking on eggshells, careful not to say something wrong. The people who met you after see you for the account, not the person. Either way, you don’t know how to answer when someone asks what you want.
content.imageDescription: You caught between two groups: old friends speaking cautiously, new acquaintances offering fake smiles, everyone watching you, waiting for a response you hesitate to give.

content.text: One night, you wander through your house. You notice you haven’t used half the rooms. The art on the walls was picked by a decorator. The kitchen is showroom clean—there are more coffee machines than mugs.
content.imageDescription: Nighttime stroll through your magnificent, mostly unused living space. You pause in a spotless kitchen with a row of appliances and unused mugs, the emptiness palpable.

content.text: You sit out on the balcony. The lights of the city twinkle, and you remember sitting on the roof of your college apartment, shivering, splitting a six-pack with your best friend, talking about everything and nothing until sunrise.
content.imageDescription: You sitting alone on your large balcony, city skyline in view. Ghostly overlay of younger you and your friend on a shabby college rooftop, sharing beers under the stars.

content.text: You call him, but he sends you to voicemail. He texts the next morning: busy with work, family stuff. You get it.
content.imageDescription: You on the balcony with your phone to your ear, hearing voicemail. Next frame: morning, you glance at his apologetic text on your phone.

content.text: Invitations to weddings slow, then stop. Some friends get married without telling you. Photos appear on social media after the fact. You realize you weren’t invited. You wonder if it’s because you’d change the dynamic. If your presence would make everyone else feel small. Or if you made promises you forgot about.
content.imageDescription: You scroll through social media, pausing at friends’ wedding photos. Expression falls as you realize you weren't there. You're alone in a luxurious but empty living room.

content.text: You try to fix things. You invite the old crew to a vacation, all expenses paid, no strings. Some say yes, but a couple say no, can’t get away, too busy. You go anyway. The ones who come gravitate toward each other, not you. They talk about things you don't remember, stories from jobs you never worked.
content.imageDescription: Beach resort setting: you're at a group table, old friends laughing with each other about inside jokes, while you sit slightly apart, trying to engage but feeling disconnected.

content.text: You pick up the check, but the table feels emptier than it should. On the flight home, you realize they didn’t take a single photo with you.
content.imageDescription: Restaurant table: you hand the bill to the waiter, a subtle sadness in your eyes as the others chat amongst themselves. Cut to an airplane seat, you staring out the window, alone.

content.text: Your life now is quiet, but not peaceful. You hear from old friends less and less. Family gatherings become formal. People avoid eye contact when the topic turns to money.
content.imageDescription: Scene of you hosting a family dinner in your grand dining room. People sit around the table, faces closed off, conversation stiff as eyes avoid meeting yours.

content.text: You host Thanksgiving in the city, hoping to reconnect. Your mother is polite. Your brother leaves early. When you ask him why, he shrugs: nothing’s the way it was.
content.imageDescription: Thanksgiving table, plates half-finished. Your mother gives you a forced smile; your brother stands, jacket in hand, shrugging apologetically as he leaves early.

content.text: You realize most of your day is meetings—account managers, wealth advisors, property maintenance. You review invitations to events, but RSVP no. You order dinner, but it’s just for one. At night, silence hangs in your high-ceilinged living room. The fridge hums, louder than you remember. You check your bank app. More zeroes than ever. It makes you feel nothing at all.
content.imageDescription: Montage of you in Zoom meetings, ignoring event invites on your laptop. Alone at a small dinner table in a vast, echoing room, scrolling your bank app with an indifferent look.

content.text: On a Friday night, you eat alone and scroll through old photos. Friends together on a couch, everyone smiling at something off-camera. Someone’s holding a slice of pizza. The walls are chipped, the sofa is old, but everyone is laughing. Those nights didn’t feel special at the time, just normal. Now, you’d trade all the Michelin stars for a plate of microwave fries and a story about someone’s dumb boss.
content.imageDescription: You sitting alone on a designer couch, looking through printed photos: vibrant past scenes of you and friends laughing on a worn-out couch, pizza boxes and stories filling the room.

content.text: You realize you stopped reaching out. At some point you told yourself it was them, but it’s you. You set the barrier. You made everything a transaction. You start to wonder if you made your world too big to be inside it.
content.imageDescription: Mirror shot: you studying your own reflection, expression pensive. Around you, luxury furnishings create a sense of isolation, hinting that your world has grown too large to feel intimate.

content.text: The next morning, you take a walk around your neighborhood. You stop at the coffee shop where you used to go every weekend, the one with mismatched chairs and the barista who wore the same hoodie every shift. No one inside notices you at first. You get in line.
content.imageDescription: Street view: You strolling casually through your old neighborhood, passing familiar landmarks, then entering a cozy, slightly cluttered coffee shop filled with mismatched chairs and busy regulars.

content.text: When it’s your turn, you order the usual. The barista recognizes you and smiles. You sit and sip your coffee. It tastes exactly the same.
content.imageDescription: Close-up: you at the coffee counter, barista brightens as they recognize you. Next, you sit at a small table by the window, taking a contented sip.

content.text: You think about texting your brother. Not to offer money, not to solve anything, just to talk. You do it. He doesn’t reply right away. In the window, your reflection is just a person, not your bank balance. You realize this is the smallest your life has felt in years. And maybe the best.
content.imageDescription: You sitting in the coffee shop, typing a casual text to your brother—just a message, no attachments. You stare at your reflection in the café window, your expression thoughtful and peaceful.

content.text: You finish your coffee, put your phone away, and walk outside, no driver, no appointment, nothing urgent. The city is loud and messy, but it feels like home in a way your luxury penthouse never did. For the first time in years, you linger.
content.imageDescription: You step out onto a bustling city sidewalk, blending into the flow of pedestrians. There’s no assistant, no limo. You pause, taking in the everyday life: people, noise, stray dogs, traffic.

content.text: You realize being rich just means having options. What you do with them matters more than how many you have. Your life doesn’t have to change. Who you are doesn’t have to be different. The simple things still matter. You remember who you used to be, and for a moment, you feel just like yourself again.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot as you wander, hands in pockets, through the city streets—smiling quietly to yourself, surrounded by ordinary moments. Sunlight and life around you, you look freer than ever.
